dataset.name: EID Quick Reference - Windows Security Mitigations (Exploit Protection)

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: ACG audit — code page modification blocked (audit mode)
entries.summary: Arbitrary Code Guard (ACG) would have blocked a code page modification but is running in audit mode.
entries.details: Generated when a process protected by Arbitrary Code Guard (ACG) attempts to modify the protection of an existing executable memory page and ACG is configured in audit (non-enforcing) mode. ACG prevents attackers from injecting shellcode by blocking modification of existing code pages and creation of new executable pages. This audit event fires when a JIT compiler or — more significantly — exploit shellcode attempts to mark a page as executable after writing attacker-controlled content. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath. Audit events (odd-numbered EIDs in this channel) indicate the mitigation is configured but not blocking. Switch to enforce mode (EID 2) to block.

investigationPivots: Identify the process via ImagePath — JIT compilers (node.exe, java.exe) legitimately need ACG excluded
If ImagePath is a browser, Office app, or unexpected process, treat as potential exploitation attempt
Correlate with process creation logs (EID 4688 or Sysmon EID 1) to see if the process was spawned abnormally
commonFalsePositives: JIT-compiling applications (JavaScript engines, Java, .NET) modifying their own code pages during normal execution
Virtualization-based applications that require dynamic code generation

entries.title: ACG enforce — code page modification blocked
entries.summary: Arbitrary Code Guard (ACG) blocked a code page modification in enforce mode.
entries.details: Generated when ACG in enforce mode blocks a process from modifying or creating an executable memory page. This is a direct block of a common shellcode injection step. The process that triggered this event attempted to mark a writable page as executable — a pattern used by virtually all user-space shellcode injection techniques after writing payload bytes. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath. An enforce-mode block on an unexpected process (not a known JIT compiler) is a strong indicator of active exploitation. The process may crash after this block; correlate with application crash events and Windows Error Reporting.

investigationPivots: An ACG block on a non-JIT process is a high-confidence exploitation indicator — escalate immediately
Check if the process crashed (Windows Error Reporting, WER) after the block — crash after ACG block confirms shellcode injection attempt
Review parent process and command line via EID 4688/Sysmon EID 1 to understand how the process was launched
commonFalsePositives: Applications that were not excluded from ACG but require dynamic code generation
Misconfigured ACG policy applied to a JIT-dependent application

detectionRules.title: ACG Enforce-Mode Block — Shellcode Injection Attempt
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Security-Mitigations"
| where EventID == 2
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="ImagePath">' ImagePath '</Data>' *
| where ImagePath !has_any ("node.exe", "java.exe", "javaw.exe")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ImagePath
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Exclude known JIT-dependent apps (node, java) via allowlist. Any remaining block is near-certain shellcode injection. Correlate with WER crash events on the same process.

detectionRules.title: ACG Enforce Mode Block
detectionRules.rule:
title: Exploit Protection ACG Block
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security-mitigations
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 2
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Applications not excluded from ACG that require JIT
level: critical

entries.title: Child process restriction audit — blocked child process creation (audit mode)
entries.summary: A protected process attempted to create a child process but the restriction is in audit mode.
entries.details: Generated when a process configured with child process restrictions attempts to spawn a child process and the policy is in audit mode. Preventing child process creation is a key defense for applications that have no legitimate need to spawn processes — particularly Office applications, PDF readers, and web browsers. Exploitation typically leads to child process spawning (e.g., cmd.exe, PowerShell, wscript.exe) to execute the next stage payload. This audit event captures those attempts without blocking. Key fields: ProcessId (parent), ImagePath (parent), ChildProcessId, ChildImagePath.

investigationPivots: Review ChildImagePath — cmd.exe, powershell.exe, wscript.exe, mshta.exe spawned from Office or PDF apps are highly suspicious
Correlate with EID 4688 or Sysmon EID 1 to get full command line of the child process
Check parent process for signs of exploitation (unusual memory, network connections)
commonFalsePositives: Macro-enabled Office documents that legitimately spawn helper processes (rare in hardened environments)
PDF readers or browsers that spawn update or helper processes

entries.title: Child process restriction enforce — child process creation blocked
entries.summary: A protected process attempted to spawn a child process and was blocked.
entries.details: Generated when a protected process is blocked from spawning a child process in enforce mode. This directly prevents the most common post-exploitation execution step: a compromised application (Word, Acrobat, browser) attempting to run a shell or script interpreter. Key fields: ProcessId (parent), ImagePath, ChildImagePath. An enforce-mode block where ChildImagePath is a shell or interpreter (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, wscript.exe, mshta.exe) spawned from a document-processing or browser application is a near-certain indicator of exploitation in progress.

investigationPivots: Treat any block of shell/interpreter child from Office/PDF/browser as active exploitation — isolate the endpoint
Preserve the parent process memory dump if possible before it exits
Check network connections from the parent process at the time of the block for C2 indicators
commonFalsePositives: Policy misconfiguration applied to an application that legitimately requires child process spawning

detectionRules.title: Child Process Blocked — Post-Exploitation Execution Attempt
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-Security-Mitigations"
| where EventID == 4
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="ImagePath">' ImagePath '</Data>' *
| parse EventData with * '<Data Name="ChildImagePath">' ChildImagePath '</Data>' *
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ImagePath, ChildImagePath
| order by TimeGenerated desc

detectionRules.title: Exploit Protection Child Process Blocked
detectionRules.rule:
title: Exploit Protection Child Process Blocked
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security-mitigations
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Policy misconfiguration on apps requiring child spawning
level: critical

entries.title: Low integrity image restriction audit — low-integrity image load blocked (audit mode)
entries.summary: A protected process attempted to load an image from a low integrity location (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when a process configured to block low-integrity image loading attempts to load a module from a low-integrity source (e.g., temp directory, user profile, browser download cache) and the policy is in audit mode. Attackers delivering malicious DLLs via browser downloads or temp directories will trigger this event when a targeted application attempts to load them. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath (loading process), and the path of the image being loaded.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Hijack Execution Flow: DLL Search Order Hijacking

investigationPivots: Check the low-integrity image path — downloads folder, temp, or AppData paths are highest risk
Correlate with browser download logs or web proxy events to identify how the DLL arrived
Review the loading process context — was it spawned from a suspicious parent?
commonFalsePositives: Applications that legitimately load plugins or extensions from user-writable directories

entries.title: Low integrity image restriction enforce — low-integrity image load blocked
entries.summary: A protected process was blocked from loading an image from a low-integrity location.
entries.details: Generated when enforce mode prevents a process from loading a module sourced from a low-integrity location. Blocks DLL injection or DLL planting attacks where the malicious DLL resides in a user-writable, low-integrity path. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath (loading process), blocked image path.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Hijack Execution Flow: DLL Search Order Hijacking

investigationPivots: Identify who placed the DLL in the low-integrity path — check file creation time, owner, and source process
Correlate with EID 11 (Sysmon file created) or equivalent for the DLL write event
commonFalsePositives: Application installers that write to temp directories and immediately load the extracted DLL

entries.title: Remote image restriction audit — remote image load blocked (audit mode)
entries.summary: A protected process attempted to load an image from a remote path (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when a process attempts to load a PE image (DLL or EXE) from a UNC path or remote share and the remote image restriction is in audit mode. Loading DLLs from remote shares is a technique used in lateral movement and for UAC bypass — for example, placing a malicious DLL on an attacker-controlled share and coercing a target process to load it. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath (loading process), and the remote image path.

investigationPivots: Identify the remote image path — an external IP or unknown share hosting a DLL is critical
Correlate with SMB client events (Microsoft-Windows-SMBClient/Operational) for the share access
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate network applications loading shared libraries from authorized file servers
Software deployment tools staging executables on network shares before loading

entries.title: Remote image restriction enforce — remote image load blocked
entries.summary: A protected process was blocked from loading an image from a remote path.
entries.details: Generated when enforce mode prevents a process from loading a PE image from a UNC or remote share path. This directly blocks remote DLL injection and lateral movement via DLL planting on network shares. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, and the blocked remote path.

investigationPivots: The remote path reveals the attacker's infrastructure — pivot on the share host for additional indicators
Identify which user/process attempted to load from the remote path
commonFalsePositives: Network application servers whose config does not exclude known remote library paths from the policy

entries.title: Win32k syscall disable audit — Win32k API call blocked (audit mode)
entries.summary: A protected process attempted a Win32k kernel API call that is blocked by policy (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when a process configured to disable Win32k system calls attempts to invoke a Win32k kernel API and the policy is in audit mode. The Win32k.sys kernel driver handles the graphical subsystem and has historically been a rich source of kernel exploitation primitives — disabling Win32k access for processes that do not need graphics (sandboxed processes, server-side services) dramatically reduces the kernel attack surface. This mitigation is used by Chrome, Edge, and other sandboxed processes. Audit events indicate the process is testing the constraint without being blocked yet. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, the specific Win32k API called.

investigationPivots: Review the specific Win32k API called — graphics APIs from non-GUI server processes are anomalous
Correlate with other suspicious activity from the same process (network connections, file writes)
commonFalsePositives: Applications that call Win32k APIs for UI rendering that were not excluded from the Win32k disable policy

entries.title: Win32k syscall disable enforce — Win32k API call blocked
entries.summary: A protected process was blocked from making a Win32k kernel API call.
entries.details: Generated when enforce mode prevents a process from calling a Win32k kernel API. This blocks a common kernel privilege escalation path: a sandboxed or restricted process using Win32k vulnerabilities to escape to kernel mode. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, and the blocked API. Enforce-mode blocks from unexpected processes (not known browser sandboxes or configured service processes) indicate either misconfiguration or an exploitation attempt.

investigationPivots: Blocks from browser sandbox processes (chrome.exe, msedge.exe child processes) during active browsing sessions are highest priority — indicates sandbox escape attempt
Correlate with any privilege changes logged in Security log after this event
commonFalsePositives: Policy applied to an application that requires Win32k access but was not excluded

entries.title: CIG audit — unsigned/untrusted image load blocked (audit mode)
entries.summary: Code Integrity Guard (CIG) would have blocked loading of an unsigned or untrusted image (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when a process protected by Code Integrity Guard (CIG) attempts to load an image that is not signed by Microsoft or a trusted publisher, and CIG is in audit mode. CIG enforces that a protected process can only load Microsoft-signed or WHQL-signed code, preventing injection of unsigned malicious DLLs. Unlike ACG (which focuses on memory permissions), CIG focuses on code provenance. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath (loading process), and the unsigned image path. In audit mode, the load proceeds but is logged.

investigationPivots: Review the unsigned image path — DLLs in temp, AppData, or non-standard paths without signatures are high risk
Check the image's digital signature (or lack thereof) and compare against known-good versions
Correlate with file write events to identify when and how the unsigned DLL was placed on disk
commonFalsePositives: Third-party security or monitoring tools injecting unsigned instrumentation DLLs
Development builds or internal tools not submitted for code signing

entries.title: CIG enforce — unsigned/untrusted image load blocked
entries.summary: Code Integrity Guard (CIG) blocked loading of an unsigned or untrusted image.
entries.details: Generated when CIG in enforce mode blocks a process from loading an unsigned or untrusted DLL. This directly prevents the most common DLL injection technique: writing an unsigned malicious DLL and injecting it into a protected process. The blocking occurs at the image load point — the DLL cannot execute. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, and the blocked DLL path. Enforce-mode CIG blocks on targeted processes (browsers, document readers) with non-Microsoft DLLs are high-confidence injection attempts.

investigationPivots: Identify the entity that wrote the unsigned DLL to disk — use file creation events to find the writer process
Check the blocked DLL hash against threat intelligence to confirm if it is a known malicious tool
Review the target process for other indicators of compromise at the time of the attempted injection
commonFalsePositives: Endpoint security products, DLP agents, or monitoring tools that inject unsigned hooks into protected processes
Applications with outdated or missing code signatures being updated but not yet re-signed

entries.title: EAF audit — Export Address Table access blocked (audit mode)
entries.summary: Export Address Filter (EAF) detected access to a sensitive export table from shellcode-like context (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when Export Address Filtering (EAF) detects a read of an export address table of a sensitive module (kernel32.dll, ntdll.dll, kernelbase.dll) from a memory region not associated with a loaded module — a pattern consistent with shellcode performing API resolution. Shellcode typically locates Windows API functions by walking the PEB/LDR structures and parsing the EAT of core DLLs. EAF intercepts these reads when they originate from non-module-backed memory. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, and the address of the EAT access. Audit mode logs without blocking.

investigationPivots: EAF firing from a document-processing or browser process is a near-certain shellcode execution indicator
Correlate with ACG events (EIDs 1/2) — combined ACG + EAF events from the same process confirm shellcode injection
commonFalsePositives: Certain DBI (dynamic binary instrumentation) frameworks and profiling tools that walk module exports
WINE compatibility layer or other non-standard PE loaders

entries.title: EAF enforce — Export Address Table access blocked
entries.summary: Export Address Filter (EAF) blocked access to a sensitive export table from shellcode-like context.
entries.details: Generated when EAF in enforce mode blocks a suspicious EAT access from non-module-backed memory. This prevents shellcode from resolving API function addresses — a prerequisite for most shellcode payloads that need to call Windows APIs. Blocking EAT access typically causes the shellcode to fail before executing its payload. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, and the blocked access address.

investigationPivots: Combine with EID 2 (ACG block) — if both fire from the same process, active exploitation is occurring
Capture a memory dump of the process before it exits to analyze the shellcode
commonFalsePositives: DBI tools or profilers that were not excluded from the EAF policy

entries.title: EAF+ audit — enhanced Export Address Table access blocked (audit mode)
entries.summary: Export Address Filter Plus (EAF+) detected suspicious access to sensitive export tables from non-standard context (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated by EAF+ (an enhanced variant of EAF) in audit mode. EAF+ adds additional heuristics beyond raw EAT read detection, including checking the call stack for suspicious return addresses or non-module-backed caller context. This catches more sophisticated shellcode that performs EAT reads from within a legitimate module's memory range (using techniques like module stomping or ROP-based EAT walking) that would evade basic EAF. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, TargetObject (module whose EAT was accessed).

investigationPivots: EAF+ firing indicates a more sophisticated exploit attempt that evades basic EAF
Correlate with ROP mitigation events (EIDs 19-24) from the same process
commonFalsePositives: Same as EAF (EID 13) — DBI tools and unusual PE loaders

entries.title: EAF+ enforce — enhanced Export Address Table access blocked
entries.summary: Export Address Filter Plus (EAF+) blocked suspicious access to sensitive export tables.
entries.details: Generated when EAF+ in enforce mode blocks a suspicious EAT access that passed basic EAF checks but triggered EAF+ heuristics. Indicates a more sophisticated exploitation attempt using ROP chains or module stomping to walk the EAT from within legitimate code space. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, TargetObject.

investigationPivots: EAF+ block combined with ROP events from the same process = sophisticated exploit in progress
Treat as critical — collect memory and disk artifacts before containment if safe to do so
commonFalsePositives: Advanced DBI frameworks with non-standard calling conventions

entries.title: IAF audit — Import Address Table access blocked (audit mode)
entries.summary: Import Address Filter (IAF) detected suspicious access to an import address table (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when Import Address Filtering (IAF) detects a suspicious write or hook of an import address table (IAT) entry from outside legitimate code context, in audit mode. IAT hooking is a classic API hooking technique used by both rootkits (for stealth) and security products (for monitoring). Malicious IAT modifications redirect API calls made by the target module to attacker-controlled code. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, and the IAT entry being accessed.

investigationPivots: Identify what IAT entry was targeted — hooks on sensitive APIs (NtOpenProcess, VirtualAllocEx) are highest priority
Correlate with process injection indicators: remote thread creation (Sysmon EID 8) or cross-process memory writes
commonFalsePositives: Security products that legitimately hook IAT entries for monitoring purposes
Application compatibility shims modifying IAT entries

entries.title: IAF enforce — Import Address Table access blocked
entries.summary: Import Address Filter (IAF) blocked suspicious access to an import address table.
entries.details: Generated when IAF in enforce mode blocks a suspicious IAT modification attempt. Prevents API hooking via IAT patching by code operating outside normal module context. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, blocked IAT access target.

investigationPivots: Check the memory region attempting to write to the IAT — non-module-backed memory writing to IAT is definitive injection
Correlate with other injection indicators from the same source process
commonFalsePositives: Security or monitoring tools that IAT-hook protected processes and were not excluded

entries.title: ROP StackPivot audit — stack pivot detected (audit mode)
entries.summary: A ROP stack pivot gadget was detected in a protected process (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when the Stack Pivot ROP mitigation detects that the stack pointer (RSP/ESP) does not point to the thread's expected stack region, indicating a stack pivot — the canonical first step of a Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) chain. ROP is used to bypass DEP/NX: instead of injecting executable code, the attacker chains together existing code fragments ('gadgets') by controlling the stack. A stack pivot redirects RSP to attacker-controlled data, converting it into a gadget address chain. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, and the pivot address. Audit mode logs without terminating the process.

investigationPivots: A stack pivot event from any non-compiler process is a critical exploitation indicator — escalate immediately
Correlate with EAF/EAF+ events from the same process — combined ROP + EAF = active chained exploit
Review the process for any unusual network activity or file writes that occurred at the same timestamp
commonFalsePositives: Certain coroutine or fiber implementations that switch stacks as part of normal execution
Custom threading libraries using non-standard stack management

entries.title: ROP StackPivot enforce — stack pivot blocked
entries.summary: A ROP stack pivot was detected and the process was terminated.
entries.details: Generated when the Stack Pivot mitigation in enforce mode detects and blocks a stack pivot, terminating the process. This is one of the most definitive exploit detection signals available from Windows — a stack pivot in a protected process is exploitation in progress. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, pivot address. The process termination prevents payload execution but means the exploitation attempt was already in flight. Treat as confirmed exploitation attempt.

investigationPivots: Isolate the endpoint immediately — exploitation was attempted and may have partially succeeded before termination
Collect the crash dump if generated — it may contain exploit artifacts
Determine the exploit delivery vector: email attachment, browser drive-by, document malspam
commonFalsePositives: Extremely rare — coroutine/fiber implementations that were not excluded from the policy

entries.title: ROP CallerCheck audit — suspicious API caller detected (audit mode)
entries.summary: A protected process made an API call where the return address does not point to a valid code region (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when CallerCheck detects that a sensitive API call (typically kernel32/ntdll API) was dispatched from a return address that does not fall within any loaded module's code section — indicating the call was made by a ROP gadget or shellcode rather than legitimate compiled code. Legitimate API calls have return addresses pointing back into the calling module. Audit mode logs without blocking. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, and the suspicious return address.

investigationPivots: Identify which API was called from the invalid return address — process injection APIs (VirtualAlloc, WriteProcessMemory) from non-module context are highest risk
Correlate with StackPivot events (EID 19/20) from the same process
commonFalsePositives: JIT-compiled code invoking system APIs — JIT code pages may not appear as standard module regions
Trampolining or thunk code in some instrumentation frameworks

entries.title: ROP CallerCheck enforce — suspicious API caller blocked
entries.summary: An API call from an invalid return address (ROP/shellcode) was blocked and the process terminated.
entries.details: Generated when CallerCheck in enforce mode blocks and terminates a process that attempted a sensitive API call from a non-module-backed return address. Prevents ROP chains and shellcode from completing API calls needed to advance the exploit (e.g., calling VirtualProtect to mark shellcode executable, or calling CreateProcess). Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, the blocked API call, and the invalid return address.

investigationPivots: Treat as confirmed exploitation attempt — isolate the endpoint
The blocked API name reveals the exploit's intent (e.g., VirtualAllocEx = shellcode staging, WinExec = direct execution)
commonFalsePositives: JIT compilers calling system APIs without ACG exception configuration

entries.title: ROP SimExec audit — simulated execution path anomaly detected (audit mode)
entries.summary: SimExec detected that a call instruction's return address does not match the expected instruction following the call (audit mode).
entries.details: Generated when Simulate Execution (SimExec) detects that a call to a sensitive API has a return address that does not correspond to the instruction immediately following the call in the calling code — a sign that the call was made via a ROP gadget (e.g., a 'call rax' gadget) rather than a direct compiled call instruction. Legitimate compiled code has call instructions whose return address is the next sequential instruction. ROP chains invoking APIs via indirect calls leave a return address pointing into the gadget's parent module, not the expected next instruction. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, the mismatched return address.

investigationPivots: SimExec is a more precise ROP indicator than CallerCheck — fewer false positives
Correlate with CallerCheck and StackPivot events from the same process for a complete ROP picture
The process image and timing relative to suspicious network or file activity help establish the delivery vector
commonFalsePositives: Unusual calling conventions in certain scripting engine JIT code paths
Obfuscated but legitimate code using indirect call patterns

entries.title: ROP SimExec enforce — simulated execution path anomaly blocked
entries.summary: SimExec blocked and terminated a process due to a ROP-based API call with mismatched return address.
entries.details: Generated when SimExec in enforce mode terminates a process that attempted a sensitive API call via a ROP indirect call pattern. This is the most precise of the ROP mitigations — it specifically detects the return address mismatch signature that differentiates ROP gadget calls from legitimate compiled calls. Key fields: ProcessId, ImagePath, the mismatched return address, and the API called.

investigationPivots: SimExec enforce block = confirmed ROP exploitation attempt — isolate the endpoint
Collect memory dump and document the full event chain (EIDs 13/14, 19/20, 21/22, 23/24) to reconstruct the exploit chain
Determine delivery vector: email, web, lateral movement — correlate with network and email gateway logs
commonFalsePositives: Extremely rare in enforce mode — nearly always indicates exploitation
